sentences.text: We saw a month before.

When the Russia war broke out, we were told among us-- nobody told us, but a rumor went around that they're going to bring the Russian prisoners of war into this camp, and we're going to be sent home.
We actually picked a date that that date, we're going to go home.
It was a Sunday morning, and the SS came in, chased us all out into the yard.
And at that date, they picked seven boys from our camp and they hanged them-- first three, then more three, then one on the end.
And there was a big public-- a lot of people came in to see that spectacle.
They were sitting on the gallery in the stadium.
And from that day on, we didn't even know what's going to happen to us.
No.
They propped up some jargon, language, in English-- in German.
We didn't even know what they were saying.
They said one stole this and one stole that.
And from that day on, we came home from work-- every time we came home, they marched us out because our camp was the main camp.
And there wasn't a day that a hanging wasn't going on from the satellite camps.
I assume they tried to scare us or that we shouldn't run off.
We were always threatened.
And there was no reason.
We didn't understand why they are doing it.
About 10 days before we left the camp, we had some satellite camps liquidated.
We were afraid where they were going to send us.
That they're going to kill us.
We didn't believe that the others were sent back home.
In fact, some of them weren't sent home.
And the night before, they came in and said, you're going home tomorrow.
They actually put us on cattle trains, and we were brought into the Lodz ghetto.
None of us could believe it's going to happen.
But I was born in Lodz ghetto.
I recognized that it's the Lodz ghetto.
Because some of us-- they were not from Lodz.
Certain little towns surrounding Lodz.
And they put us in a holding place in the ghetto, which was watched by Jewish police-- ghetto police.
And they called it [INAUDIBLE].
It was a prison.
But we were not watched.
We were not treated as prisoners.
But they had to have a place to keep us.
Before, we were taken to a bath and cleaned up.
We were filthy from A to Z.
It took me a couple of days to stay there because my name was Zeidenfeld, and they took us by alphabet.
There was one little incident.
When I came back from Poznan, I had some bags, old clothes, and I brought it with me.
But being from Lodz, I could go out through the wires and see if I can find some in my family.
I went around a whole day and couldn't find anybody.
Coming back, I missed a piece of bread, which I never got it, and my bags were gone.
I thought the whole world is coming to an end.
But soon did I found out they didn't mean anything.
And the second day, I was taken to the bath, and cleaned up, and they gave us some other clothes-- not new clothes.
It was used clothes.
And they asked me if I have any family in the ghetto.
And the day I went out before, I stumbled on somebody, and he told me that they know less or more where my father lives, but they didn't know about my mother.
And I told him, yes, I have a father, not knowing if I do or don't, because I had to have a place that I go to.
And they let me out.
And I did find my father.
But when I came toward the door, there was a lock hanging, and nobody was there.
So I knocked on the next door, and I asked a neighbor if a man by this and this name lives, and he says, yes, but he's still at work, and he should be home any minute.
So I asked if I could stay and wait.
He says, yes.
And I heard the door open up.
A few minutes later, I knocked on the door, and I came in.
My father did not recognize me.
I was away already three and a half years.
I got older.
I was skinny, looked shabby.
My father wasn't in very good health either.
And we started talking, and I told him that I come from a camp, which I bring regards from his son.
And after a while, I told him it's me, and we both broke down, and we hugged each other.
And he told me what happened to the family-- that my mother died several months before from starvation.
In the ghetto, they had to work seven days a week.
There was no medicine, no hospitalization.
And he told me the whole story from the rest of it, and I got tired and went to bed.
But before I went to bed, he told me that I got to find a place of work, because in the ghetto, everybody works.
No.
He told me about my sister.
He told me that the sister was taking [INAUDIBLE] children.
But they never heard of my brother.
They all assumed that those kids were killed.
At that time, no.
I did not know in the Lodz ghetto.
Yes.
He mentioned to me that a cousin by the name of Voreich-- they used to own a lumber yard-- that he is foreman of a particular kommando, which had taken apart old buildings.
And I should go up to him, and he would place me on the job.
And I wouldn't have to work hard, because I couldn't.
And that's the way it happened.
The following day, I went to work.
We had a little pot for soup hanging on the back of our belt.
And I got nice and dirty.
I couldn't do much work.
And I worked, I think, about three days.
And he comes over to me and tells me that he heard a rumor that the ones who came a few days ago from Poznan camp are being rounded up by the Jewish police.
And he suggested to me to go out and hide until it gets dark, because I had to cross a bridge over an area where, underneath, was not the ghetto, where the streetcar went, and where my father worked.
And I should not go in the daytime because at the bridge, the police might stop me.
What I did-- I went into an outhouse, and I waited there until it got dark.
And I went over to the bridge, and I came close to the house.
There were two policemen waiting for me.
I did not go into the house because I knew my father is cooking.
There was a special, place-- little place where people cooked their [? real ?] meal in the evening, because there was no other means of cooking in the house.
I went down and I found my father, and I told him the story.
And he said to me, you know, you have a good friend in this district, [INAUDIBLE].
Number 6.
Why don't you go there?
And this thing will blow over a few days.
Once they get 1,000 people, they'll send them out, and whoever stays in the ghetto will stay.
I did it.
And the next day, evening, I did go over the bridge again to meet my father.
And I met my father.
And he gave me some bread because I couldn't get any rations.
Whatever he had, he shared with me.
Because of the family estate, they didn't have much to offer me.
And I went back to sleep.
The next day, again, I came over.
My father was not in the place, and there was no police at my house.
I didn't know what happened.
So I went into the house and knocked at the door [INAUDIBLE] neighbor.
And they told me that my father was taken out last night by the police, by the Jewish police, because they couldn't find you.
So I said to myself, what's the sense?
I've been away already over three years.
How long can it go on?
I went over to the police station.
And I told them, actually, you don't need my father.
You want me.
Release my father, and I'll stay.
And that's the way it happened.
They did release my father.
And I was taken into another holding place.
And I day later, we were shipped out to Auschwitz.
Yes, I was.
I just said to him, goodbye.
I don't know where I'm going, but I hope it's not going to be too long.
He hugged me.
I mean, he was a man of [INAUDIBLE].
What can he say to a 14-year-old boy?
By cattle car.
I think it was between two and three days.
We were going back and forth and forth and back.
We were given a piece of bread going on and not much [? laterwards. ?] The facilities were terrible.
We had one pail.
And people at night died.
We slept on dead people.
It was a mess.
It was only men.
No, I didn't know.
It was dark.
I didn't know who was next to me.
The train stopped in those days in Auschwitz, not in Birkenau.
We were unloaded.
And it was several big trucks, more than several.
And we were separated by left, right, right, left.
We didn't know what's going to happen to them and what's going to happen to us.
In fact, I said to somebody is  standing next to me, I says they must be-- going to have a better job.
They're taking them on trucks, and we're going to march.
We are probably going to get harder labor than they do.
We didn't know we were going.
We did.
No, they didn't.
They only helped us go out.
And whatever we had for belongings, they took away from us.
They said, throw it there.
Didn't have much.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I do remember there was a story going around of 1,000 people, 180 went into the camp.
When we came into the camp, the first thing was that we were put in a barrack where they gave us tattoos.
And my number-- a number was given to me by one of the people who were in [INAUDIBLE] like we were-- one of the prisoners.
And they tattooed a number on me.
Once the number was given, we were marched to a place where they have showers-- cold showers.
Our clothes was taken away.
And we had had a shower-- whatever shower was available.
Went out on the other side.
It was pretty cold.
It was March.
And that area of Poland was very wintery yet.
And we were given other rags.
A tall guy got a short rag and a short guy got a tall rag, a short sleeve, big shoes.
We sort of interchanged with each other.
And we made the best of it.
And we were marched into a block, a barrack, which they called the quarantine.
They kept you there for a couple of weeks until we were assigned to a barrack, and assigned also to a kommando, which was where people went to work.
Oh, yes.
I was first in block 11.
Then I was in block-- barrack 16.
In barrack 16 I was a long time, until one day, in the place where I was working, there were civilian Poles working.
They were building some sort of sewer system for the camps.
And we did the dirty work, bringing the morter, the bricks.
And one day, a civilian tapped me on my shoulder, not knowing that he was a German, and asked me my number.
And he wrote it down, not knowing why or what's going to happen to me.
In the evening, when I came back to Birkenau at the camp, usually is a [INAUDIBLE] appel, which they sent us out in fifths.
And until they counted everyone was there, they didn't let us into the barracks.
And my number was called out.
They did not call us by name, only by numbers.
And a German said that I was-- in German, he said that I was-- I was a speck of lint, and I tried to carry on with the black market with the civilian people.
And I was given 25 lashes and six months of hard labor in the camp, separated from all other prisoners, in a separate barrack.
Yeah, 25 lashes.
They lay you down on a horse, right in the middle where everybody stands around-- the whole block, the whole barrack-- 1,000 people.
And they tie your feet and hands, stretched out, and they give you lashes.
It wasn't an assessment.
It was a sign from the camp-- a German.
I don't know who he was.
But they had their [? henchmen. ?]
Very sore.
Very sore.
It took a long time.
I remember I came back to the barracks that we had some water where we tried to cool it off, but that's all.
We did not get any medicine or anything.
You always found somebody in the camp that had a little pity on you and tried to help you among ourselves.
Several weeks.
Definitely.
I worked.
But again, I did what I could.
Instead of him watching me, I watched him.
When the SS men walked away, I stood.
And when he walked around, I picked up a shovel.
The work was immaterial.
We didn't do nothing-- anything of substance.
Our kommando was watched strictly by SS men, because of the hard labor given out.
They had big dogs, too.
The food situation wasn't any worse or any better what the other people got.
In the morning, we got a piece of bread.
[INAUDIBLE] cover-- what they call in German 'the black water.' And at lunch, they gave us a soup.
If you could find a few [? peels ?] of tomato, it was a thick soup.
It wasn't much of a food.
And at nighttime, we got the same soup.
But, you know, some people helped themselves through other friends, what they worked in the kitchen or they worked in certain places.
I didn't have that much luck.
But somehow, I got a piece of bread from here and there.
We helped out, within the camp, each other, especially with this kommando, where everybody was one of the special prisoners.
Yes, this was a barrack.
We could not have any communication with the outside of this barrack with the rest of the inmates in the camp.
The same as the other barracks, yeah.
The only bad part of it was in the morning, if you didn't get up in time, you had a hose of water on your head.
And this was not by Germans.
It was done also by some of the leaders from the camps-- prisoners.
But they picked such roughnecks that they didn't have to do the dirty work.
Mostly German.
They were criminals.
Because they had-- we had a Magen David on our [INAUDIBLE].
They had a green triangle.
And when they had a green triangle, we knew they were thieves or the lowest-- the lowest.
Some were Poles, too.
I did not stay too long there.
I stayed there between two and three weeks.
Because in April, they were getting ready to bring in the Hungarian Jews.
And one morning, they rounded up most of the Jews from that barrack, strafkommando barrack, that prison, because there were other nationalities in the prison, too.
And they took us into the next block, which was the sonderkommando.
The sonderkommndo was the ones who worked the crematoriums.
This was 1943, April-- middle of April-- '44.
Pardon me, it was '44.
I was already in the camp a little over a year.
I know what it meant, because now being in camp over a year, we had our stories.
It's not a question of how you feel.
I had no choice.
I didn't know what tomorrow was going to bring.
I saw the Hungarian transports coming in in masses.
My heart was-- couldn't take it.
One incident was how I got initiated to this kommando was terrible.
In Auschwitz, we had four crematoriums-- in Birkenau.
One in Auschwitz, and four in Birkenau.
Before the crematoriums were built, they actually had pits, what they call a place [?
Brzezinka. ?] Not far away.
And in those days, they had three huts-- wooden huts, straw roofs, with a door on the front and a door on the back.
And the capacity was between 250 people in each of those huts.
They gassed them in those little huts.
And behind the huts about 300, 400 yards were those pits, shaped in a V.
After the gassing, I was introduced to this particular job, which was out of this world.
I didn't know what to do with myself, in the mud on the ground.
We took those bodies out and loaded them on trucks-- two-wheel trucks, which they tip one way or the other, and dragged them over to the pits.
And we brought them close to the pits, and we tipped those bodies into it.
It was a horrible night.
I don't know how long I worked there.
The hours were endless.
[INAUDIBLE] this was that night-- very little light.
But I'll never forget the second day with the incident.
That a transport came in, and there were small children.
And they took some of those children, and they threw them in-- into the pits-- living children.
It was beyond my imagination.
I didn't know-- I just collapsed.
We worked there two nights, day and night, with very little food.
And finally, they brought us back to camp.
When they brought us back to camp, there was a rumor going around that we're going to be living in the crematoriums on top of the ovens-- second floor.
At that time, the kommando was 800 people.
And the second day-- we rested up one day-- they actually brought us into the crematoriums, and they assigned us rooms where to stay and sleep, which was a little better than in the camps.
We did not have straw.
We actually had boards what laid out some blankets.
And our blankets to cover, and only two tiers-- one on top of the other.
And we worked 12-hour shifts.
And being the youngest among the group, I was assigned by the Jewish kapo to clean the quarters of the Germans, which worked there in the offices.
Polished their boots.
I used to darn their socks.
And luckily, I did not work too hard.
But I saw more misery than anybody else because they put people into a group.
One did that, one did this.
I had to run around both crematoriums, because being so young and servicing them, they had-- in each crematorium, they had offices.
And in the one place, there was a laboratory.
So I had a red bandanna on my arm that I could cross the street.
This was the camp street.
But nobody could cross that street.
But fortunately, I had seen more things than some of the other-- my other friends-- did.
And this only lasted until October, 1944.
